text: The Nike Women's Marathon hit the streets of San Francisco this weekend with over 30,000
racers taking part in half and full marathons, including two of our PopSugar editors, Michelle
Foley and Molly Goodson.
And they are here with post-race recovery tips for the next time your body takes on
some serious mileage.
So Michelle, you ran the half, Molly you ran the full.
How are you guys feeling today?
I feel good.
I'm a little sore, but I feel really good.
About the same.
All right, I'll just take your word for it on that.
All right, so everyone really front loads in terms of preparation for a race like this.
You really need to put in the time.
But after the race, you cross that finish line, you need to follow some tips.
What did you guys do as soon as you finished?
I think the first thing to keep in mind is that your emotions are going to be running
really, really high when you cross the finish line.
And you should not immediately beat yourself up or start getting down yourself if you didn't
reach that goal time that you were aiming for or something happened along the way that
sort of slowed you down.
Do not think about that.
Just be real.
Really pride yourself in that moment.
You accomplished something.
And there's always another race if you want to get back out there.
Yeah, and going off of what Molly said, emotions are high at the finish line.
It's really easy to skip the cool down, which is one of the worst mistakes I think you can
make.
Take 10 minutes to stretch.
Walk around.
You will be so happy you did this the next day when you wake up.
It is true.
Resist the urge to curl up and a little ball on the ground.
So, you know, enjoy the celebratory atmosphere.
Don't beat yourself up, but make sure and do your cool down.
Yes.
So, obviously you're going to be faced with some food options at the end.
Perhaps some of your friends have brought you some stuff or there's going to be stuff
at the finish line.
What should you choose to eat after a race like this?
It's important to eat after your race.
If you've run a half marathon, you've probably burned upwards 1,500 calories.
You're looking for something with a good amount of proteins and a good amount of carbs that's
going to help fight off muscle soreness.
I particularly like nut butter sandwiches with banana and a side of chocolate milk.
So you basically have a kid's meal.
I do.
I do.
I think that's very cool.
And on the more adult side of things, I am a big proponent of rewarding yourself.
A lot of races have beer gardens at the end, so I think it's fun to enjoy a little post-race
beer.
It's got carbs.
It's got calories.
It's a great way to celebrate with fellow runners.
But definitely keep in mind that you are on an empty stomach and there's a lot of adrenaline,
so it will hit you pretty fast.
Moderation.
Go slow.
Ease into it.
Also ease back into foods like maybe fried foods or candy or whatever it is you maybe
gave up right before the race.
Don't just like shove it all in your face.
So don't chug like four beers and eat an entire cake.
No.
Probably not.
Chug water.
Okay.
You can't chug water.
Chug water.
So just make sure you're totally hydrated.
Now the muscles, that's going to be a big piece of all this.
You're just fatigued and you've put them through the ringer.
What should you do to alleviate that?
Yeah, muscle soreness is huge in any sort of long exercise activity.
If you can, treat yourself to a nice massage.
It helps with lactic acid buildup.
It fights muscle fatigue.
And if you're like me and you are really sore and you get a lot of impulsivity, you're going
to get inflammation, ice.
You know, 20 minutes on the effective area on and off all day long.
It's really going to help.
It's important also in the days after the marathon as your muscles start to feel better
not to immediately push yourself to get back out there.
I know the urge will be there, but keep it light.
Go for a really light run or swim or bike or try something else because you hear too
many stories about runners getting back out there too fast and that's when they get injured
and that's not what you want to happen.
Yeah.
No, after you've made it across the finish line, you certainly don't want to, you know,
harm yourself after the race.
Now, you guys have finished it.
Was it worth it?
Would you do it again?
I think, yeah, I would absolutely do it again.
Yeah, I'll get back at you.
Okay.
Well, with these post-race tips, you'll be ready to do it.
Thanks so much, guys, and congratulations.
Thanks.
Thanks.
